title: Impurity-induced geometric correlations and fractional quantization in quantum Hall systems
abstract: We propose a geometric mechanism for fractional quantum Hall states based on impurity-induced correlations within a Landau level. A correlated distribution of ionized impurities partially modifies the Landau-level degeneracy through coherent coupling between cyclotron orbits, generating fractional energy sublevels. The odd-denominator hierarchy emerges naturally from the intrinsic guiding-center quantization and the correlated cyclotron motion. The resulting spectrum reproduces the principal experimentally observed fractional sequences and predicts a strong dependence of fractional-state stability on impurity geometry and layer separation. The absence of an incompressible Hall plateau at filling factor 1/2 follows from cancellation of the geometric correlations responsible for odd-denominator states. These results suggest that impurity-induced geometry may constitute an additional organizing principle in quantum Hall systems.
summary: This paper proposes that the fractional quantum Hall effect hierarchy arises from geometric correlations between cyclotron orbits induced by impurities. This mechanism provides a new way to understand the emergence of fractional energy levels and the stability of specific filling factors. It suggests that impurity geometry is a fundamental organizing principle in quantum Hall systems.
main_problem: Investigating whether impurity-induced spatial correlations and geometric aspects can serve as an organizing principle for the formation of fractional quantum Hall states.
main_result: The author proposes a geometric mechanism where correlated impurities induce coherent coupling between cyclotron orbits, generating a fractional energy spectrum that reproduces the observed odd-denominator hierarchy and explains the absence of a 1/2 plateau.
method: A phenomenological framework using the effective-mass approximation and symmetric gauge to derive an effective fractional energy spectrum from the interplay of guiding-center quantization and correlated cyclotron motion.
model_or_system: A two-dimensional electron gas (2DEG) in a strong perpendicular magnetic field, subject to a potential from a correlated distribution of ionized impurities located at a finite distance from the 2DEG plane.
key_observables: Hall conductivity (sigma_xy) plateaux and longitudinal resistivity (rho_xx) minima.
important_parameters: Landau level index (n), fractional hierarchy parameters (p, q), dimensionless correlation parameter (eta), and impurity-2DEG separation (Delta).
main_assumptions: Effective-mass approximation, symmetric gauge, nearest-neighbor approximation for impurity potential matrix elements, and the association of guiding-center radius with a geometric quantization scale.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a schematic of impurity-induced correlated cyclotron orbits; Figure 2 and 3 display predicted fractional plateaux in Hall magnetoconductivity and corresponding minima in longitudinal resistivity for the 1/3 and 1/5 families.
paper_structure: The paper introduces a geometric mechanism for FQH states, defines the physical model and Hamiltonian, derives the fractional energy spectrum through orbital correlations, compares the predicted hierarchy to experimental sequences, and discusses the stability of states based on impurity geometry.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Schematic representation of impurity-induced correlated cyclotron orbits in a 2DES.

FIG. 2: (a) Hall magnetoconductivity σxy showing the fractional plateaux associated with the 1/3 family. (b) Longitudinal magnetoresistivity ρxx (Shubnikov–de Haas response) exhibiting the corresponding fractional minima predicted from the fractional spectrum obtained from Eq. (17).

FIG. 3: (a) Hall magnetoconductivity σxy showing the fractional plateaux associated with the 1/5 family. (b) Longitudinal magnetoresistivity ρxx (Shubnikov–de Haas response) exhibiting the corresponding fractional minima predicted from the fractional spectrum obtained from Eq. (17).
